exemplar.title:
: What is the future of the Earth-Bennu system
with asteroid Bennu posing a future threat to Earth having its
closed elliptical orbit intersect that of Earth?

: What is the future of the Earth-Bennu system
with asteroid Bennu posing a future threat to Earth having its
closed elliptical orbit intersect that of Earth?

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, narrowly focused and consistently revisited throughout the essay, anchoring calculations and discussion to the investigationâs aim.
Methodology is comprehensive and wellâjustified, employing appropriate mathematical tools (parametric equations, rotation matrices, orbital mechanics) and authoritative data sources.
Calculations, graphs and discussions all tie back to the central question, demonstrating coherent alignment between method and purpose.
weaknesses: Some phrasing ambiguities in the research question and occasional background digressions dilute the sharpness of focus.
Late acknowledgment of model limitations (neglect of perturbations, constantâvelocity assumption) suggests the methodological scope could have been more tightly bounded.
Minor overprecision in reported numerical details (e.g. rotation angles to four significant figures) indicates uneven treatment of methodological precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs investigation consistently orients around a narrowly defined research question and employs a wellâstructured, justifiable methodology. Minor linguistic ambiguities and digressions prevent a fully flawless presentation of focus and method, placing performance solidly at 5/6.

strengths: Exhibits excellent subject knowledge, applying conicâsection geometry, parametric representations and celestial-mechanics formulae accurately and effectively.
Terminology (perihelion, aphelion, eccentricity, sphere of influence, hyperbolic trajectory) is used consistently and correctly throughout.
Software tools (GeoGebra, Desmos) are integrated smoothly into quantitative analysis, reinforcing the rigour of computations.
weaknesses: A few minor unitâconversion slip-ups and typographical inconsistencies occur, but these do not materially undermine the overall clarity or correctness.
Inâtext citations are sometimes incomplete, slightly diminishing scholarly traceability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates consistently strong and accurate use of subject-specific knowledge and terminology, with only isolated minor lapses. This level of performance merits the top band (6/6) for Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Analysis is substantial and coherent: derivations of orbital parameters, 3-D modelling and SOI entry calculations are logically structured and well supported.
Research quality is high, integrating NASA data, peer-reviewed literature and credible astronomical databases to inform modelling choices.
Discussion reflects reasoned argumentation, acknowledges model assumptions and limitations, and proposes directions for further study.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation of data sources and numerical methods is somewhat superficial; alternative models and quantitative uncertainty analysis are not fully explored.
Some conclusions rely on graphical approximations rather than more precise algebraic or numerical methods.
The evaluation of satellite-collision risk remains largely qualitative, lacking quantitative support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs critical thinking shows clear, reasoned analysis and solid research quality, with partial critical evaluation of methods and results. Performance lies in the upper part of the 7â9 band, warranting 9/12 overall.

strengths: Report structure follows IB expectations impeccably (title page, contents, numbered sections, conclusion, references), facilitating reader navigation.
Figures, tables and equations are clearly labelled and cross-referenced, supporting comprehension of complex derivations.
Formatting requirements (word count, title, page numbers) are fully met.
weaknesses: Occasional lowâresolution screenshots and mixed figure orientations slightly disrupt visual consistency.
Extended blocks of unsimplified equations could be streamlined for readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is clear, well-organized and fully meets formatting and structural standards, with only minor layout issues that do not impede understanding. This merits the maximum score of 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is presented prominently on the title page and restated in the introduction.  â¢ It is narrow enough to be investigated mathematically (timing of SOI entry, velocity comparison, deflection analysis) and remains the thread that organises the body of the essay.  â¢ Calculations, graphs and discussion all refer back to answering âWhat is the future of the Earth-Bennu system â¦?â showing strong linkage.  â¢ Some phrasing ambiguity ("future of the system" could be interpreted broadly) and the late recognition of model limitations show that the focus, while clear, is not perfect; therefore a high but not maximum score is appropriate.

The analysis demonstrates **substantial depth and coherence**:  
1. Derivation of orbital parameters, coordinate transformations and time-dependent equations are logically linked to the research question.  
2. The student carries the analysis into 3-D modelling, calculates entry time into Earthâs SOI, escape velocities and deflection geometry, synthesising physics and mathematics.  
3. Conclusions follow from calculations and limitations are acknowledged.  
A few steps rely on simplifying assumptions (e.g., ignoring perturbations, constant velocity inside SOI) and some results are obtained graphically rather than analytically, so the analysis is âmostlyâ rather than âfullyâ effectiveâhence an 8/12.

Throughout the paper the student uses subject-specific languageâperihelion, aphelion, eccentricity, sphere of influence, escape velocity, hyperbolic trajectoryâaccurately and confidently.  â¢ Mathematical notation for vectors, parametric equations and trigonometric relations is largely correct and consistent; diagrams are clearly labelled with conventional symbols.  â¢ Minor lapses (e.g. inconsistent symbol definitions, occasional typographical errors) are present but do not impede comprehension.  Overall usage is accurate and sustained, warranting a score of 5/6.

â¢ The student provides word count, title, page numbers, labelled figures and consistent citation style, satisfying most IB formatting requirements.
â¢ Diagrams are clear; tables and equations are neatly typeset.  Some screenshots are low-resolution and the mix of portrait/landscape figure orientations occasionally disrupts visual coherence.  
â¢ Given these minor lapses, the work meets the descriptors for the top band but not flawlessly; a score of 3/4 reflects solid, if not perfect, layout execution.

The essay exhibits **robust mathematical knowledge**: conic-section geometry, parametric representations, rotational matrices and basic celestial-mechanics formulae are applied correctly.  â¢ Most computations are sound and generally lead to plausible quantitative results; the student even checks model output against published NASA estimates and discusses discrepancies.  â¢ Occasional slip-ups (unit conversions, large time-shift mis-labelled as âyearsâ) and reliance on approximate graphical solutions show that knowledge/application is not uniformly exemplary, so the strand sits comfortably at 5 rather than 6.

The studyâs title, introduction and concluding sections leave no doubt that the essay examines the prospective dynamical evolution of the Earth-Bennu system.  â¢ The student contextualizes the threat of near-Earth asteroids, explains why Bennu is significant, and explicitly states the purposeâto determine when and how Bennu might interact with Earth.  â¢ Section headings and repeated references to the guiding question keep the reader aware of the investigationâs focus.  â¢ Minor issues of wording (e.g. a rather long, compound research question) and occasional digressions into descriptive background prevent the communication from being flawless, hence a score of 5 rather than 6.

The report follows the expected EE structureâtitle page, abstract-equivalent introduction, table of contents, sequentially numbered sections, conclusion and referencesâwhich **strongly supports reader comprehension**.  Cross-referenced figures, equations and appendices (graphs, screenshots) are integrated logically.  Minor issues (some overly long sections and occasional repetition) slightly affect flow but do not hinder understanding; therefore, the highest band (3-4) is attained with a score of 4.

â¢ The student gathers a broad range of authoritative sources (NASA mission data, peer-reviewed articles, astronomical databases) and integrates them effectively into the modelling process.
â¢ Quantitative data (orbital parameters, physical constants) are used appropriately; computations are mostly correct and transparent, showing verification via software graphs.  
â¢ Some reliance on secondary websites of uneven academic rigour and limited critical appraisal of data uncertainty prevent the work from reaching the âconsistently excellentâ level, so it fits the 7-9 band at a solid 8.

The discussion interprets mathematical findings, relates them to satellite-collision risk, and reflects on discrepancies with NASA predictionsâshowing **reasoned argument and partial critical evaluation**.  â¢ Strengths: explicit consideration of model assumptions, acknowledgment of limitations (neglect of perturbations, axis alignment), and suggestions for further study.  â¢ Weaknesses: evaluation of reliability is brief, alternative models are not explored quantitatively, and the implications section leans toward speculation without rigorous support.  The work thus aligns with the 4-6 band; a mid-high score of 5 is appropriate.

The methodology is comprehensive and well justified:  â¢ The student derives parametric equations for both orbits, applies rotation matrices, includes inclination, and calibrates initial conditions using discovery-date dataâdemonstrating informed selection of mathematical tools (conic sections, orbital mechanics, 3-D transformations).  â¢ Use of software (GeoGebra, Desmos) is appropriate and documented, and data sources (NASA, literature on Bennu, Earth orbital parameters) are cited.  â¢ Simplifications (neglecting perturbations, assuming constant velocity inside SOI) are acknowledged but somewhat diminish completeness; overall the approach is coherent and suitable, meriting 5/6.

annotations.comment: Solving graphically for the discovery-date shift introduces approximation error. An algebraic or numerical root-finding method could yield more precise determination of tâ for improved model fidelity.
The Case Study section effectively frames your methodology by clearly stating the approach to deriving orbital equations, demonstrating a coherent research plan.
Reliance on an unvetted online 3D model (Space Reference) without critical appraisal of its accuracy may introduce uncertainties. Evaluate source reliability or cross-check with peer-reviewed data.
The conversion of Earthâs SOI radius from kilometres to astronomical units ($r_E\approx0.006177\,$AU) is correctly executed, enabling coherent use within the orbital distance equation.
The presentation of two full pages of unsimplified equations impedes readability. Consider summarising intermediate steps and providing final streamlined expressions for clarity.
Assuming the inclination axis coincides with the x-axis from a visual cue requires stronger justification or quantitative evidence, as even small misalignments can affect 3D modelling accuracy.
Using Desmos to compute derivatives of orbital equations for velocity components demonstrates technical proficiency and effective integration of software tools in quantitative analysis.
The stepwise plan to rotate Bennuâs orbit first in 2D, then apply inclination, demonstrates logical structuring of the methodology and clear alignment with the research question.
Reporting the rotation angle to four significant figures (76.6769â¦) suggests false precision. Rounding to three significant figures (76.7Â°) would suffice given model assumptions.
Deriving the deflected trajectoryâs eccentricity via $e=1/\sin\varphi$ is mathematically sound and shows advanced application of hyperbolic geometry to gravitational scattering.
The inclusion of the title at the very start of the report satisfies IB formatting requirements and immediately situates the investigation, demonstrating attention to structural conventions.

The research question is clear but overly long and compound, which may obscure its focus. Consider simplifying language to state: âWhen and how will asteroid Bennu first enter Earthâs sphere of influence?â to sharpen scope.
Accurate calculation of Earthâs semi-major and semi-minor axes using perihelion and aphelion distances shows precise application of astronomical data and unit conversion.
The word count is clearly stated at the beginning, meeting IB requirements and ensuring compliance with the 4,000-word limitâexcellent attention to administrative detail.
The detailed table of contents provides a clear roadmap, aiding navigation through complex sections and supporting reader comprehension of the reportâs structure.
In-text citations such as â(âMotion of Celestialâ¦â)â are incomplete and impair traceability. Use full author or source names consistently to strengthen scholarly rigour and clarity.
The introductory overview of conic sections is thorough but digresses from the central focus. Consider condensing background to maintain sharp alignment with the research question.
The diagram placing the Sun at the origin and defining the AU as unit is well chosen. It shows accurate subject knowledge and effective use of coordinate systems for orbital modelling.
The parametric equations for an ellipse are presented accurately in LaTeX, reflecting strong command of mathematical notation and clear conceptual understanding.
The figure clearly labels axes and key elements, facilitating comprehension of elliptical characteristics. This effective visual integration supports reader understanding.
The escape velocity conversion ($11.2\,$km/s to $0.006469\,$AU/day) is accurate, reflecting clear understanding of unit relations and facilitating direct comparison with Bennuâs entry speed.
The magnitude calculation of Bennuâs velocity at entry ($v_e\approx0.016099\,$AU/day) is correctly derived from component values, showing methodical application of vector norms.
The schematic of hyperbolic deflection within Earthâs SOI is well drawn and effectively annotated, providing a clear conceptual framework for subsequent mathematical derivations.
There is a minor inconsistency: BEâs magnitude used in the dot-product differs slightly from the stated SOI radius. Ensure BE is consistently computed as $r_E$ when applying geometric relations.
The discussion of satellite risk is compelling but qualitative. To strengthen evaluation, include quantitative estimates of satellite density and collision probability to support risk claims.
